q: Alpha decay ($\alpha$ decay) is a process in which an atomic nucleus emits an alpha particle (helium nucleus) and transforms into a different atomic nucleus, with mass number reduces by four and atomic number reduces by two.
In alpha decay, the mass number of the parent nucleus is reduced by two.
Atomic number and mass number are conserved in the decay process.
$\beta^-$ decay (beta-minus decay) is a process in which a neutron in an atomic nucleus changes to a proton with the emission of an electron ($\beta^-$) and an antineutrino ($\bar{\nu}_e$).
In $ eta^-$ decay, the parent nucleus transforms into the daughter nucleus, with mass number remains unchanged but atomic number increases by one.
$\beta^+$ decay (beta-plus decay) is a process in which a proton in an atomic nucleus changes to a neutron with the emission of a positron ($\beta^+$) and a neutrino ($\nu_e$).
In $ eta^+$ decay, the mass number of the parent nucleus increases by one.
Positron, the antiparticle of electron, has the same mass and same magnitude of electric charge as an electron.
Positron is negatively charged.
The existence of positron was theoretically predicted by Dirac in 1931 and experimentally detected by Anderson in 1932.
Neutrino, a fundamental particle as an electron, has no charge and negligibly small rest mass ($ < 2 \times 10^{-37}\text{ kg}$).
Each neutrino is associated with an antineutrino.
For a nucleus to be stable, there must be an appropriate ratio of protons and neutrons.
If a nucleus has excess neutrons, $\beta^+$ decay takes place.
A nucleus with excess protons undergoes $\beta^+$ decay.
In gamma decay ($\gamma$ decay), the daughter nucleus resulting from alpha or beta decay is left in an excited state and it can then decay to a lower energy state by emitting a gamma ray photon.
In gamma decay, the atomic number and mass number of the parent and daughter nuclei remain unchanged.
Gamma decay takes place when a nucleus at an excited state deexcites to a lower excited state or to the ground state.
Photons are massless and move at the speed of light in vacuum.
The energy of a photon is directly proportional to the wavelength of the electromagnetic radiation associated with it.
Alpha rays consist of helium nuclei, positively charged particles.
An $\alpha$ particle consists of two protons and two neutrons.
The charge of an $\alpha$ particle is $+2 e$.
Alpha rays are the least ionizing of the three rays.
Alpha rays are the least penetrating of the three rays.
Alpha rays can be stopped by a thick sheet of paper.
Beta rays consist of either electrons (charge $= -e$) or positrons (charge $= +e$) moving with varying speeds.
Beta rays are more ionizing than alpha rays.
Beta rays can be stopped by a few millimetres of aluminium.
Gamma rays are electromagnetic waves like light and X-rays, but have much shorter wavelengths.
Gamma rays have no charge.
Gamma rays can be deflected by electric and magnetic fields.
Intensity of gamma rays can be greatly reduced by several centimetres of lead but they are never completely stopped.
The rate of decay of a radioactive sample is called activity.
SI unit for activity is becquerel (Bq).
$1\text{ Bq} = 1\text{ event s}^{-1}$ (or 1 disintegration $\text{s}^{-1}$).
A unit of activity still being used today is curie (Ci).
The rate of decay (activity) is a characteristic of the radioactive element and it is affected by the temperature.
Activity of a radioactive sample decreases with time.
The half-life is defined as the time for half the atoms in a radioactive sample to decay.
Half-life of radioactive substances ranges from about femtosecond ($10^{-15}\text{ s}$) to billions of year.
Radioactive decay is a statistical process: it is impossible to predict when a particular atom will decay.
A radioisotope is an atom with an unstable nucleus which undergoes radioactive decay, resulting in the emission of alpha, beta and gamma rays.
A tracer is a small amount of radioactive isotope introduced into a system in order to follow the behaviour of some components of that system.
In medicine, radioactive tracers are effectively used in diagnostic procedures.
Treatment with radiation is known as radiotherapy.
Radioisotopes such as cobalt-60 and cesium-137 are widely used to treat cancer in radiotherapy.
In industrial gamma radiography, radioactive materials are used to inspect metal castings or welded joints.
Radioactive dating is a technique for estimating the age of an object by measuring the amounts of various radioisotopes present in it.
Carbon dating is a technique, developed by Willard Libby, for estimating the age of the remains of plants and animals.
The abundance of carbon-14 in the atmosphere is essentially constant.
There is one carbon-14 atom in every trillion ($10^{12}$) of carbon-12 atoms.
Lead is the stable final decay product of a series of decays that starts with uranium.
Gamma and neutron irradiation of seeds can be used to induce mutations to develop desired mutants.
Nuclear energy now provides about 10 % of the world's electricity from about 450 nuclear power reactors.
A nuclear reaction is a process in which a particle or a gamma radiation penetrate into a nucleus and changes its configuration.
In an exothermic reaction, the total mass before a nuclear reaction takes place is greater than the total mass after reaction.
In an endothermic reaction, the total mass before a nuclear reaction takes place is less than the total mass after reaction.
Nuclear fission is a process where a neutron strikes and penetrates a nucleus of uranium-235, and the nucleus splits into two lighter nuclei.
For a chain reaction to be maintained, the uranium-235 has to be above a certain critical mass.
The energy released per atom in a nuclear reaction is about a million times greater than the energy released per atom from a chemical reaction.
Nuclear reactor is a device in which a fission chain reaction can be initiated, maintained and controlled.
The essential elements of a nuclear reactor are fissionable nuclear fuel, moderator, coolant, control rods, reactor vessel and shielding.
Uranium-235 or plutonium-239 can be used as fuel in a nuclear reactor.
Moderator is a material used to speed up fast neutrons released from a fission process.
Graphite and heavy water ($\text{D}_2\text{O}$) are used as moderators.
Coolant is used to extract the heat produced by fission fragments.
Water and carbon dioxide $\text{CO}_2$ gas are used as coolant.
Control rods are used to control the rate of reaction by absorbing neutrons.
Boron and cadmium are used as control rods.
Shielding (or containment structure) is a protective barrier, usually a dense material, that reduces the passage of radiations by absorbing them.
Materials which are radioactive and for which there is no further use are called radioactive waste.
After three or four years in a reactor, spent fuel must be removed and replaced with a new one.
Strontium-90 becomes concentrated in bones and iodine in the thyroid gland.
Plutonium is highly toxic; if breathed in, a very small amount can kill a person.
In an atomic bomb, a controlled chain reaction takes place.
The detonation of an atomic bomb releases enormous amounts of thermal energy, or heat, achieving temperatures of several million degrees Celsius.
Spent nuclear fuel storage is mostly a problem regarding environmental concerns.
Greenhouse gas emissions from nuclear fission power are much larger than those associated with coal, oil and gas.
In 1905, Albert Einstein proposed that light must also be thought of as particle or photon.
Louis de Broglie suggested that the momentum $p$ of a particle and its associated wavelength $\lambda$ are related by the equation $\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$.
The wavelength $\lambda$ associated with a moving particle (matter wave) is also called de Broglie wavelength.
All moving particles exhibit properties of both particles and waves nature.
The existence of matter waves were first experimentally confirmed by Thomson’s cathode ray diffraction experiment and the Davisson-Germer experiment for electrons.
In electron microscope, the wave nature of electrons is used instead of light.
An integral number of de Broglie wavelengths associated with a revolving electron must fit in the circumference of an orbit.
De Broglie hypothesis proves Bohr’s second postulate, which states that the angular momentum of the orbiting electron is quantized.
Wave nature can be easily observed for macroscopic particles like a basketball.
1 eV (electron volt) is the energy gained by an electron when accelerated through a potential of one volt.
Galileo was the first to point out that all motion is relative.
The choice of the frame of reference is arbitrary.
During the 19th century, it was generally believed that light could not travel across empty space.
Ether was an invisible hypothetical material supposed to fill space and through which light waves traveled.
Michelson and Morley found a shift in the fringes of the interference pattern when detecting the motion of the earth through ether.
The Michelson-Morley experiment result is generally considered to be the first strong evidence against the prevalent ether theory.
Einstein’s special theory of relativity is based on two simple postulates.
The laws of physics are the same in all inertial frames of reference.
The speed of light in vacuum is affected by the speed of its source.
An inertial frame is a frame of reference that has no acceleration.
All objects in an inertial frame follow Newton’s first law of motion.
Two events that are simultaneous in one frame of reference may not be simultaneous in another frame moving relative to the first frame.
Time dilation is the phenomenon of time passing more slowly for an observer who is moving relative to another observer.
Each inertial observer determines that all clocks in motion relative to the observer run slower than that observer’s own clock.
Relativistic effects usually become significant when speeds become comparable to the speed of light.
Proper length $L_0$ is the length of an object measured by an observer who is moving relative to the object.
When an object is moving with speed $v$ parallel to its length, its length is measured to be shorter.
Rest mass $m_0$ is measured when a particle is at rest relative to the observer.
Newton’s second law of motion is valid in relativistic mechanics if relativistic momentum $\vec{p}$ is introduced.
A rapidly moving particle increases in mass.
The law of conservation of energy is valid relativistically.
The total energy of an object of mass $m_0$ at rest is $E_0 = m_0 c^2$.
Special Relativity was published in 1905.
General Relativity is a theory of gravity.
Special Relativity applies to observers in non-inertial frames.
